title: Trump justifies striking Iran: What You Need To Know
author: ABC News
fullText: This is what you need to know now. President Trump offers a new explanation for striking Iran, denying Israel forces hand. It comes as the war escalates overseas with the US shutting more embassies across the region and urging Americans to get out. >> So, we truly are living in a nightmare here. >> An American living under bombardment in Beirut describes the nightmare of daily strikes and sleepless nights. A major sell-off on Wall Street. Investors reacting to growing fears the conflict could widen while gas prices spike in just one day. Plus, a small plane makes a dramatic emergency landing in the Hudson River. And the two people on board survive. And a teen driver trapped in a river after a crash rescued just in time thanks to her Apple Watch and quick acting deputies. We have much more what you need to know now. I'm Rachel Scott from ABC News. It is Tuesday, March 3rd. This is what you need to know. Let's get right to the big story. The war between Iran, the United States, and Israel is now intensifying. Both sides have expanded air strikes with at least 11 countries now involved in the conflict. At least six US service members have been killed. Across Iran's capital, explosions rang out throughout the night into Tuesday. Now, the White House under scrutiny for offering different explanations for why the US struck Iran. President Trump first pointed to Iran's missile capabilities, >> the long range missiles that can now threaten our very good friends and allies in Europe, our troops stationed overseas and could soon reach the American homeland. >> Then Defense Secretary Pete Hegv said the strikes were to prevent Iran from advancing its nuclear weapons. >> Iran had a conventional gun to our head as they tried to lie their way to a nuclear bomb. Secretary of State Marco Rubio has offered another justification, saying the US struck first to prevent expected retaliation after an anticipated Israeli attack. >> We knew that there was going to be an Israeli action. We knew that that would precipitate an attack against American forces, and we knew that if we didn't preemptively go after them before they launched those attacks, we would suffer higher casualties. >> And now, for the first time since the war started, the president took questions publicly from reporters in the Oval Office on camera today. I asked the president about the shifting rationale and he seemed to offer up a new explanation. >> Did Israel force your hand to launch these strikes against Iran? Did Netanyahu pull the United States into this war? >> No. I might have forced their hand. Uh you see, we were having negotiations with these lunatics and it was my opinion that they were going to attack first. They were going to attack. If we didn't do it, they were going to attack first. I felt strongly about that. The president also told me he was surprised Iran targeted neutral countries, accusing Thrron of striking nations that were helping negotiate and even hitting civilian sites. He called the regime evil and argued something had to be done after decades of violence. The US has now carried out nearly 2,000 strikes on Iran across at least 50 locations. President Trump says US munition stockpiles have never been higher, calling supplies virtually unlimited. He said the US is prepared for the war to continue whatever it takes, adding that conflicts can be fought, quote, forever using current inventories. President Trump did acknowledge some of the highest grade munitions are not where we want them to be, blaming the previous administration support for Ukraine. Meanwhile, the State Department says it has added Kuwait, Qatar, and Iraq to a list of countries where diplomatic missions are being evacuated or operations are being reduced. And the US embassy in Saudi Arabia is now closed after Saudi officials say it was struck by Iranian drones causing minor damage. The US embassy in Kuwait is also shutting down after what officials described as an attack. And now the embassy in Beirut is closing too. Ian panel is across the border in Tel Aviv. >> Rachel, it's day four of this war and already it's escalating. A second front opening up inside Lebanon. attacks from Hezbollah here in Israel and attacks on US facilities and embassies across the region. The US closing some embassies, issuing an urgent warning for US citizens to leave parts of the region. Remember, Iran warned that there would be this kind of retaliation. It publicly posted it on official website saying that it would attack US assets and facilities in the region. And as they predicted, we're now seeing this growing escalation. Meanwhile, the State Department says it's preparing military and charter flights for Americans who want to leave the Middle East. It comes as we hear from one American caught in the war zone. A US citizen living in Beirut, Lebanon, says the blast happened just minutes from his home and they have nearly made it impossible to resume life as normal. >> Lowflying drones uh all throughout the day uh and periodic strikes. We're not sure when they're really going to happen. I haven't been able to sleep very well within the last 3 days. Uh only a few hours per night just because we're getting awakened in the middle of the night sometimes by loud explosions. Um and just being caught off guard. It seems the situation is only getting worse. Uh so we truly are living in a nightmare here. >> The State Department is now warning of serious safety risks as options to depart become limited due to flight cancellations and airport closures. Back here in the US, Wall Street is tumbling on Tuesday with the Dow plunging about 1,200 points after the opening bell. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq were also down more than 2% as investors react to growing fears the war with Iran could widen and weigh on the global economy. At the same time, gas prices surged just one day after the strikes on Iran, raising new concerns about the economic impact here at home. Elizabeth Schelsey breaks it down. Rachel, the war is having an immediate impact on the prices we pay. Starting at the gas pump. According to Gas Buddy, the national average is now above $3 per gallon. It's up more than 13 cents in just one day amid surging oil prices if oil prices stay elevated. Analysts say it will lead to higher overall inflation. For example, companies might pass down higher fuel costs to consumers. This threatens President Trump's claim that inflation is improving at a time when many voters say the cost of living is their top issue in the midterm elections this year. The president has also been touting lower mortgage rates, but the market turmoil is now leading to higher borrowing costs. The average 30-year fixed mortgage back above 6%. So, the bottom line, the conflict overseas directly puts at risk some bright spots in the affordability crisis here at home. >> And we have much more of what you need to know. So stay with us. Here's your daily briefing. Other big stories we are following on ABC News. Today is primary day in North Carolina, Arkansas, and Texas. All eyes are on the Texas Senate race. GOP incumbent John Cornin is facing state attorney general Ken Paxton and six others. and Democrat Jasmine Crockett and James Terico are in a dead heat match. Jurors in Georgia have convicted the father of a high school shooting suspect. The jury found Colin Gray guilty on all charges, including seconddegree murder and involuntary manslaughter. Gray was accused of providing his teenager with the rifle and the bullets used in the deadly attack at Appalache High School, which killed four and injured several others. A small plane crashed into an icy Hudson River Monday night. about 60 mi north of New York City. The two people on board swam to shore. The governor calls it another miracle on the Hudson, referring to the passenger plane that landed in the Hudson in 2009. And a 19-year-old woman is lucky to be alive after a terrifying crash in Ohio. Police say that Andy Burns was driving home from work Sunday when Slick Road sent her truck careening into a ravine and flipping into a river. The crash detection on her Apple Watch automatically called 911 as water quickly filled the vehicle. >> How far is the water? >> My head's barely up. >> Okay. Is there any way you can get your window rolled down? >> Burns was trapped with just a 4in pocket of air near her floorboards. Deputies were able to locate the truck, smash out a window, and pull her to safety. Incredibly, she survived with only a few bruises. And new today, Homeland Security Secretary Christy Gnome is on Capitol Hill for two days of testimony, facing questions from lawmakers over her handling of immigration enforcement. Gnome refused to apologize for calling Alex, who was fatally shot by a border patrol agent in Minneapolis, a domestic terrorist. Gnome said she relied on information from agents on the ground, but top ICE and Border Patrol officials, who also provided congressional testimony, said they did not give her that assessment. Gnome's appearance comes as much of the Department of Homeland Security remains unfunded in a partial government shutdown amid a funding stalemate over immigration policy, leaving key agencies like the TSA and FEMA in limbo. And finally, before you go, fans of Zootopia 2 will be able to stream the billiondoll box office sensation very soon. The hit sequel from our parent company, Disney, will hit Disney Plus Wednesday, March 11th. That's just days before the movie goes up for an Oscar at the 98th Academy Awards streaming live on Hulu. And that's what you need to know. For breaking news and live updates throughout the day, check out ABC News streaming on Disney Plus. New episode streaming every day on Disney Plus.
